title: Building and Maintaining an Approved Supplier List
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How to build and maintain an approved supplier list that satisfies EU GMP, GDP and the MHRA: risk-based qualification, scope, change control and review.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner showing a pharmaceutical quality manager's hands cross-referencing a controlled supplier register on a tablet against a shelf of neatly labelled material containers in a softly lit warehouse-cleanroom interface, cool teal and cyan tones deepening into navy shadows with a subtle green accent glowing from a status indicator, soft natural side light, shallow depth of field with creamy bokeh, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, calm precise and authoritative atmosphere.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an approved supplier list and supplier qualification?
faqs.a: Supplier qualification is the documented process of assessing a supplier through questionnaires, certificate review, risk assessment and, where justified, an audit. The approved supplier list is the controlled register that records the outcome of that process and confirms which suppliers are authorised for supply. The list must always be traceable back to the underlying qualification evidence; an entry without that supporting trail is not a control.

faqs.q: How often should an approved supplier list be reviewed?
faqs.a: Periodic re-qualification should be set on a risk basis under ICH Q9, with high-risk suppliers reviewed more frequently than low-risk ones. However, the calendar should not be the only trigger. Significant deviations, complaint trends, recalls, a change of manufacturing site or an adverse regulatory inspection should each prompt an immediate review of the supplier's status on the list.

faqs.q: Does an approved supplier list apply to GDP wholesale dealers as well as manufacturers?
faqs.a: Yes. Under the GDP guidelines enforced by the MHRA, wholesale dealers operating a WDA must verify that they obtain medicinal products only from authorised suppliers and distribute only to authorised customers. Maintaining a controlled approved supplier list of qualified, authorised sources is the practical way to evidence that obligation, just as manufacturers use it under EU GMP.
